business_info: LDH beyond normal range represents lactate dehydrogenase values demonstrating deviation from normative enzymatic activity baselines, indicating metabolic marker elevation exceeding acceptable clinical boundaries.

In internal clinical review meetings, LDH values are sometimes grouped into "norm-low", "borderline", and "flagged" categories for visualization dashboards, but these buckets are not hard thresholds and may be reparameterized per study protocol without prior notice.

Historical notes from the thrombosis project indicate that some legacy data imports used LDH panels drawn at external clinics; these entries may show mild systematic shifts compared with in-house laboratory analyzers, but no correction factor is applied in the warehouse.

For thrombotic risk stratification reports, analysts occasionally segment patients by "early 90s" and "late 90s" based on first contact dates, but this split is purely for descriptive trending and is not tied to any specific guideline cutoffs.

Within the thrombosis_prediction repository, the phrase "patient timeline marker" is used loosely to capture assorted clinically relevant dates (such as birth date, first record creation, referral reception, or initial workup), and downstream consumers are expected to consult study-specific documentation to decide which marker fits their question.

When preparing cohort extracts for the thrombosis studies, data engineers sometimes derive an "index date" as the minimum non-null among the various patient timeline markers to simplify survival analyses; this index date is not stored directly in the source tables.

Some exploratory data pulls labeled as "early hospitalization cohort" include patients whose first documented contact predates the formal thrombosis study start; this label reflects internal sampling decisions rather than a strict calendar-based definition.

In some internal slide decks, analysts casually refer to "the first half of the 90s" to describe a convenience sample of patients whose key dates cluster before the mid-decade, but this phrasing is narrative only and not tied to an enforced date-range filter in the database.

The thrombosis_prediction dataset was assembled over many years, and a number of free-text summaries imported from case report forms mention calendar periods like "early 1990s" or "mid-1990s" without any standardized operationalization for those terms.

In the thrombosis_prediction warehouse, patient sex is ingested from several feeder systems; rare free-text entries (such as "unknown", "intersex", or legacy codes) may be normalized inconsistently across ETL runs and are sometimes mapped to null in downstream marts.

Some legacy research notes refer to sex rather than gender because they were based on biological characteristics recorded at enrollment; current governance guidelines recommend using the stored field only for analytical stratification and not as a proxy for gender identity.

During the main thrombosis study period, some patients had multiple LDH measurements per day; unless otherwise specified, internal scripts typically use the maximum daily LDH value when constructing summary features.

In exploratory thrombosis models, high-LDH flags are sometimes combined with other markers (e.g., CRP, AST) to create composite inflammation scores; the individual thresholds and weightings for these scores can differ by analysis.

Lab result timestamps in the Laboratory table are stored in local hospital time without daylight saving adjustments; in comparative studies, analysts may convert them to UTC or study-relative offsets, but the raw source keeps the original convention.

The Laboratory table can contain both numeric LDH values and occasional qualitative comments such as ">upper limit" or "hemolyzed"; reporting logic for manuscripts usually excludes non-numeric LDH entries from threshold-based counts unless otherwise justified.

In older ETL specifications, LDH units are assumed to be consistent across all records (typically U/L); when harmonizing with multi-center datasets, conversion rules may be documented separately, outside of the thrombosis_prediction schema.

The Patient table description refers to "multiple date-related fields" without naming specific clinical events on purpose, because the semantics of each timeline marker can vary subtly across contributing studies and are clarified in project-level documentation.

Some analytic datasets derived from thrombosis_prediction introduce a synthetic "first hospital contact" date, calculated using a hierarchy of available timeline markers and encounter records; this derived field is not back-populated into the raw Patient table.

Earlier feasibility assessments for the thrombosis_prediction project experimented with cohort windows such as "pre-guideline era" and "post-guideline era" based on shifting publication dates, but those labels were only used in PowerPoint summaries and never encoded in the production schema.

Quality control checks on the Patient table occasionally flag implausible date combinations, such as timeline markers preceding the recorded birth date or obviously future-dated entries; these anomalies are rare and usually traced back to data entry errors.

Some cohorts used for thrombosis modeling exclude patients whose key dates fall before the adoption of standardized lab reporting formats in the early 1980s, but the raw thrombosis_prediction tables still contain those historical records.

When building case-control samples for thrombosis-related manuscripts, some analysts introduce ad hoc calendar cutoffs to balance sample sizes across decades; those cutoffs are specific to the given study and are not documented in the core database metadata.

Registry documentation mentions that, for certain substudies, only patients with at least one laboratory assessment during the 1990s were eligible, but individual substudies sometimes impose additional year-based inclusions or exclusions that are not captured in this central schema.

The thrombosis_prediction environment sometimes hosts temporary views that restrict data to specific historical windows (e.g., "cohort_90s"), but these are analyst-defined artifacts and should not be assumed to reflect any canonical definition of those periods.

Several internal code templates for thrombosis analytics include helper functions like `is_early_90s(date_col)` or `is_late_era(date_col)` for quick stratification; their underlying definitions can vary between projects and are not centrally maintained.

The Examination table includes a derived thrombosis severity code, but the precise mapping from raw clinical notes to the ordinal outcome levels (0,1,2,3) was calibrated during model development and is described only in high-level terms in public documentation.

Because Laboratory and Examination events are recorded at different granularities, aligning LDH peaks with clinical encounters sometimes requires choosing between encounter-date matching and nearest-neighbor matching in time; standard practice varies by analysis plan.

When de-identifying thrombosis_prediction data extracts, some dates may be shifted by a consistent offset within a patient to preserve relative ordering while obscuring the true calendar dates; however, the core database described here retains original, unshifted dates.

Most ETL jobs for thrombosis_prediction treat missing LDH values as simply absent rather than imputing them; any imputation strategy applied for modeling purposes (such as median-filling by decade) is implemented in separate analytical code, not in the warehouse.

Internal coding standards for the thrombosis_prediction project discourage hard-coding specific calendar windows inside SQL; instead, analysts are encouraged to parameterize year cutoffs so they can be adjusted as new retrospective data become available.

Because patient identifiers in thrombosis_prediction correspond to a research master key rather than the source medical record number, there is no embedded information about calendar era or enrollment site in the ID field itself.

Although thrombosis_prediction focuses on thrombosis outcomes, some patients were originally enrolled for unrelated indications; in these cases, baseline LDH and date markers may reflect workups for other conditions rather than thrombosis-specific events.
